author: Amrit
content:
Every developer needs a portfolio - a place to showcase projects, share ideas, and establish credibility. But where do you host it? A $5/month VPS? Vercel? Netlify?

I went a different route: **I built and deployed mine entirely on AWS using serverless services.** No servers to manage, no monthly bills to worry about (it costs me less than **$1/month**), and infrastructure defined entirely in **Terraform**.

In this post, I will walk you through exactly how I did it - from the architecture design to the Terraform code to the CI/CD pipeline that deploys it automatically on every push to `main`.

---

## The Architecture

Before diving into code, here is the high-level view:

```
User -> Route53 (DNS) -> CloudFront (CDN) -> S3 (Static Files)
                |                |
            ACM (SSL)     API Gateway -> Lambda -> DynamoDB
                              |
                          Cognito (Auth)
```

**Frontend layer:**
- **S3** - stores the React build output (HTML, CSS, JS, images)
- **CloudFront** - global CDN that serves the files with caching and HTTPS
- **Route53** - DNS routing for my custom domain `amrit.cloud`
- **ACM** - free SSL/TLS certificate for HTTPS

**Backend layer (for the blog engine):**
- **API Gateway** - RESTful endpoints for CRUD operations
- **Lambda** - Python serverless functions handling all business logic
- **DynamoDB** - NoSQL database storing blog posts, users, and comments
- **Cognito** - user authentication (sign up, sign in, email verification)

The entire infrastructure is codified in **Terraform modules**, and the deployment is automated with **GitHub Actions**.

---

## Step 1: S3 - Where It All Lives

The S3 bucket is the foundation. It stores the production build output from our React app. But here is the key: **we do not enable S3 static website hosting.** Instead, we let CloudFront handle everything and keep the bucket completely private.

```hcl
# S3 Bucket for React App
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "frontend_bucket" {
  bucket = var.domain_name
}

# Block ALL public access - CloudFront OAC handles access
resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "frontend_bucket_pab" {
  bucket                  = aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.id
  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}
```

The bucket policy only allows CloudFront to read from it:

```hcl
resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "cloudfront_policy" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "AllowCloudFrontServicePrincipal"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
        }
        Action   = "s3:GetObject"
        Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.arn}/*"
        Condition = {
          StringEquals = {
            "AWS:SourceArn" = aws_cloudfront_distribution.cdn.arn
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}
```

> **Why not enable S3 website hosting?** Because with CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC), your bucket stays completely private. No one can access it directly - all traffic goes through CloudFront, where you get caching, HTTPS, and edge locations worldwide.

---

## Step 2: CloudFront - The Global CDN

CloudFront sits in front of S3 and serves your site from edge locations around the world. It handles HTTPS, caching, and - critically for React SPAs - custom error responses that redirect 404s back to `index.html`.

```hcl
resource "aws_cloudfront_distribution" "cdn" {
  enabled             = true
  is_ipv6_enabled     = true
  default_root_object = "index.html"
  aliases             = [var.domain_name, "www.${var.domain_name}"]

  origin {
    domain_name              = aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.bucket_regional_domain_name
    origin_id                = "S3-${aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.id}"
    origin_access_control_id = aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control.default.id
  }

  default_cache_behavior {
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "S3-${aws_s3_bucket.frontend_bucket.id}"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
    min_ttl     = 0
    default_ttl = 3600
    max_ttl     = 86400
  }

  # SPA Routing: Redirect 404/403 to index.html
  custom_error_response {
    error_caching_min_ttl = 300
    error_code            = 403
    response_code         = 200
    response_page_path    = "/index.html"
  }

  viewer_certificate {
    acm_certificate_arn      = aws_acm_certificate_validation.cert_validation.certificate_arn
    ssl_support_method       = "sni-only"
    minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021"
  }
}
```

The **custom error responses** are essential for React Router. When a user navigates to `/blogs/my-post`, CloudFront looks for that path in S3 and gets a 404. The custom error response catches that and serves `index.html` instead, letting React Router handle the routing client-side.

---

## Step 3: SSL Certificate with ACM

AWS Certificate Manager provides free SSL certificates. The trick is that CloudFront requires the certificate to be in **us-east-1**.

```hcl
resource "aws_acm_certificate" "cert" {
  domain_name               = "www.${var.domain_name}"
  subject_alternative_names = [var.domain_name]
  validation_method         = "DNS"
  provider                  = aws.us-east-1

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}
```

> **Pro Tip:** The `create_before_destroy` lifecycle rule is crucial. Without it, Terraform would try to destroy the old certificate before creating a new one, causing downtime.

---

## Step 4: Route53 - Custom Domain

The final piece: pointing your domain to CloudFront.

```hcl
resource "aws_route53_record" "root_a" {
  zone_id = aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = var.domain_name
  type    = "A"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_cloudfront_distribution.cdn.domain_name
    zone_id                = aws_cloudfront_distribution.cdn.hosted_zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = false
  }
}
```

Both `amrit.cloud` and `www.amrit.cloud` resolve to the same CloudFront distribution.

---

## Step 5: Terraform Modules - Keeping It Organized

All of this infrastructure lives in reusable Terraform modules:

```
infra/
|-- modules/
|   |-- frontend/          # S3, CloudFront, Route53, ACM
|   |-- backend/           # Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, Cognito
|   \-- email/             # SES for transactional emails
\-- live/
    |-- root.hcl           # Terragrunt root config
    \-- prod/              # Production environment
```

The `live/` directory uses **Terragrunt** for DRY configuration and remote state management:

```hcl
remote_state {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket         = "amrit-portfolio-terraform-state-prod-amrit990"
    key            = "${path_relative_to_include()}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "amrit-portfolio-terraform-locks-amrit990"
  }
}
```

---

## Step 6: CI/CD with GitHub Actions

A single push to `main` triggers the entire pipeline:

```yaml
name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  infrastructure:
    steps:
      - name: Terraform Format Check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
      - name: Run tfsec Security Scanner
        uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3
      - name: Terragrunt Apply
        run: terragrunt apply -auto-approve

  frontend:
    needs: infrastructure
    steps:
      - run: npm ci && CI=true npm test -- --coverage
      - run: npm run build
      - name: Deploy to S3
        run: aws s3 sync build/ "s3://amrit.cloud/" --delete
      - name: Invalidate CloudFront Cache
        run: aws cloudfront create-invalidation --paths "/*"
      - name: OWASP ZAP Security Scan
        uses: zaproxy/action-baseline@v0.15.0
```

---

## Cost Breakdown

| Service | Monthly Cost |
|---|---|
| S3 (storage + requests) | ~$0.05 |
| CloudFront (data transfer) | ~$0.10 |
| Route53 (hosted zone) | $0.50 |
| ACM (SSL certificate) | Free |
| Lambda (invocations) | ~$0.01 |
| DynamoDB (on-demand) | ~$0.10 |
| Total | ~$0.76/month |

---

## Lessons Learned

1. **Start with Terraform from day one.** Even if it seems like overkill for a simple site, you'll thank yourself later.

2. **CloudFront custom error responses are essential for SPAs.** Without them, every client-side route returns a real 404.

3. **ACM certificates must be in us-east-1 for CloudFront.** This catches everyone the first time.

4. **Use Origin Access Control (OAC), not Origin Access Identity (OAI).** OAI is the legacy approach; OAC is more secure.

5. **Automate everything.** Once your CI/CD pipeline is set up, deployments become a `git push`.

---

## What's Next?

In the next post, I will dive into the **backend layer** - how I built a serverless REST API with API Gateway, Lambda, and DynamoDB to power the blog engine you are reading this on right now.

If you found this helpful, hit the <3 button below and drop a comment with any questions. Happy building! :)
summary: A complete guide to deploying a portfolio website on AWS using S3, CloudFront, Route53, ACM, and Terraform - with a full CI/CD pipeline powered by GitHub Actions.
title: How I Built a Serverless Portfolio on AWS - From Zero to Production
